content.assessment: A no-new-policy baseline driven purely by demography does not engage with tracking age, comprehensive provision, or political durability at all; it is a counterfactual rather than a reform. Judged against the lens's substantive criteria, it offers no structural change comparable to the Finnish comprehensive reform and, per the lens's own logic (informal sorting substituting for formal structure), passive contraction is likely to replicate the pattern observed in Finland where reduction in one dimension (school size/capacity) does not resolve underlying segregation drivers, which can persist or worsen through geographically uneven effects and continued growth of exempted providers (church schools), analogous to how Finnish school-choice segregation persisted despite formally comprehensive structure.
opportunities: Could reveal, by comparison, how much of any observed future changes in segregation are attributable to demographic drift versus deliberate reform, sharpening evaluation of structural options like T3
strengths: Provides an analytically honest counterfactual baseline against which structural interventions can be measured, methodologically useful even though it is not itself a reform
threats: As with Finland's own experience of persistent school-choice-driven segregation despite comprehensive structure, passive demographic contraction is likely to leave, or worsen, non-demographic segregation drivers (church-sector growth, Roma segregation) entirely unaddressed (F-live-finnish_reform-09)
weaknesses: Contains no mechanism addressing tracking age or comprehensive provision, the lens's central criteria
No engagement with political durability, since it involves no active policy choice to sustain or defend
